description_full:
The Community House,
Grays, Essex.
RM17 6DR.
As always, our community cafes are completely free and for all people within the autistic and wider autism community i.e. Autistics of any age, parents, carers, other family members, professionals and any mix of the aforementioned.
The main aim of our community cafes are to provide the community a safe, inclusive and accessible place for people to come together, meet, connect and share.
There will be free tea, coffee, biscuits and other snacks, sensory items, games and more!
There's no need to register, just drop by.
For a full list of our Community Cafes go to our website here: https://www.londonautismgroupcharity.org/community-cafes

name: NAS Croydon Branch
description_full:
NAS Croydon Branch
Louise Jones
Branch Chair
tel: 07833 293263,
http://www.autism.org.uk/directory/resources/12588.aspx
The National Autistic Society (NAS) Croydon Branch Parent Group aims to reduce some of the isolation felt by those who have received a diagnosis or are waiting to receive a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder for a family member. We provide: monthly coffee morning; advice and information; the opportunity to meet other parents and carers in similar circumstances; and a friendly and supportive environment. Please contact us for more information. We run an evening social group once a month for parents and carers to meet up in a relaxed and informal setting.

name: Ladbroke Grove Autism Centre
description_full:
At Ladbroke Grove autism centre we support people of all ages on the autism spectrum to help them achieve their full potential.
Social activities
We offer evening social groups for young people and adults on the autism spectrum, and a Saturday club for children aged five to 12 years old.
Evening social groups
Our social groups offer young people and adults the opportunity to develop social and communication skills, have fun, and meet other people on the autism spectrum. We organise a wide range of social events at the centre and in the community.
Saturday club
Our Saturday club for children aged 5 to 12 runs every week. The focus is on having fun while learning social and communication skills through sensory integration and play. Club membership operates on a rotating basis and each child has the opportunity to come for three months at a time.
For further information or to make a referral:
Tel: 0203 326 1200
Email: se.services@nas.org.uk
73C St Charles Square,London W10 6EJ

name: AS Mentoring
description_full:
Our Mission is to support our clients to achieve their potential as individuals and to facilitate their social inclusion through employment.
https://asmentoring.co.uk/
There are a range of services including:
For Individuals:
Pre-employment support,
Workplace support and Coaching,
For Employers:
Recruitment, Consultancy, Management,
For Families, Individuals and Employers:
Various Workshops run throughout the year.
https://asmentoring.co.uk/what-we-do
Locations
The AS Mentoring team are based in 3 areas of the UK: London, Surrey and Birmingham.
We are very aware of the need for specialist support across the UK and further so we do work with clients remotely via Skype, phone-calls, email or a combination of all. If there is a need, staff can travel to other areas of the UK on an ad-hoc basis for Workplace Support and Coaching; this is something we'd be happy to discuss with you individually.

name: Care Trade
description_full:
http://www.care-trade.org/
Tel: 020 7060 5706
email: info@caretradeuk.org
CareTrade believes passionately that all young people should be given the opportunity to reach their potential and lead fulfilling lives. Just 15% of adults with autism are in employment – its our mission to change this. One of the ways we are tackling this issue is by offering support to help adults with autism gain the skills, confidence and experience they need to succeed in employment.
Answers for Autism Projects
REACH
CareTrade has a new employment project for people aged 25 years or over, who are on the autistic spectrum and who have been unemployed for 6 months or longer.
The Autism Project
This is a Full time 2 year employment programme for young people on the autism spectrum who would like work but need more confidence, support, skills and experience. We use a module based learning system so students can start at the beginning of any term and can progress at a pace that best meets their individual needs.
The School House Café
This is a 23 seat cafe set in a lovely Victorian building adjacent to Spa School. It provides high quality food and coffee to the community as well as work experience for young people with autism.

name: Ardeley- Work Experience and Family Visits at Church Farm
description_full:
Welcome to Church Farm
Open everyday at 9.00am. Parking is free. Buy a Farm Day Pass to explore the Woodland Play Area, indoor Little Farmers Room and our Feed the Animals Farm Trail. You can see 100's of Cattle, Sheep, Pigs and poultry and naturally grown fruit and vegetables.
Also based at the farm is Rural Care. We provide training and work experience for people with learning disabilities and/or mental health issues in a supportive and caring environment on a fully working farm. We also work in partnership with North Hertfordshire College to provide accredited training for their students with learning disabilities on the farm. Volunteers are welcome and can help out with Rural Care or can work on their own so they can enjoy the tranquility of the farm.

name: Camden- Alexandra College
description_full:
Alexandra College
Ainsworth Way
London NW8 0SR
Alexandra College: No Limits Programme
Courses for Students with Learning Disabilities or Difficulties
Westminster Kingsway College offers provision for young people aged 16 to 25 with severe and complex needs that is delivered all year round in a newly refurbished centre in Camden. The new Alexandra College has been re-modelled to ensure that the learning and living space is fully accessible.
Curriculum
The No Limits Programme curriculum will focus on health, employment, independent living and community inclusion and the learning is underpinned by specialist support. Programmes will be flexible and individualised, focussing on what the students need to learn to support their transition from school to adulthood and prepare them for what they want to do next. As part of their Individual Learning Plan, students may access courses and vocational training opportunities at other Westminster Kingsway College Centres including catering, creative arts, health and beauty. Most importantly each learner will have a stimulating, meaningful and exciting time in college.
Entry requirements
Alexandra College is open to learners who are aged 16 to 25 years old (cannot be over 24 years old at the start of their final year at college), have a significant learning disability and may also have a physical disability.
Applications are invited from those who are unable to have their needs met by a school sixth form or general further education college programme for Learners with Learning Difficulties and Disabilities (LLDD) and have an Education, Health and Care Plan naming the provision (previously had a statement of educational needs or a learning disability assessment (LDA)).
This includes learners who:
-Have a severe learning disability or profound and multiple learning disabilities and may also have a physical disability
-May have an autism diagnosis
-Want to continue with their education
-Require a programme built around their individual needs
How to apply and further information:-
Places at Alexandra College must be agreed by your home borough (the borough in which you live) as the local authority funds places. Families cannot fund placements directly. Each young person for whom the local authority (in the borough where you live) agrees to consider a placement will be invited for an individual assessment to help decide if this is the right provision. To arrange this assessment, please contact the special educational needs (SEN) service in your borough.
For further information about Westminster Kingsway College’s provision for students with learning difficulties or disabilities, please contact the Programme Manager for Learners with Learning Difficulties and Disabilities at Westminster Kingsway College via 0870 060 9800 or email courseinfo@westking.ac.uk.

name: Crawley- A Potential Diamond
description_full:
We take the most natural approach for each individual. We get to know their families, communities and employers within easy reach of home. We focus on what positive attributes our jobseekers can bring to the workplace whilst embracing what adjustments the employer might need to make to live in harmony with the autism or learning disability.
Our model of employment support puts the emphasis on getting the job-fit right. Each one of our jobseekers will have their own unique set of circumstances that will make up the best job-fit. And we believe we do a very good job understanding exactly what these are.
Email:richard@apotentialdiamond.org
Phone:077389 41415.
Richard Lamplough has had, and continues to have, a varied career. For much of the last twenty years he's used his creativity, drive and communication skills to support people with autism, Asperger's Syndrome or mild learning disabilities into paid employment.

Aldenham Country Park
Aldenham Country Park consists of 100 acres of land surrounding a 60 acre lake. It has a farm with rare breed livestock such as Berkshire and British Lop pigs, Red Poll and Longhorn cattle, Black Welsh Mountain Sheep and free range poultry. All of this enables us at Rural Care to get involved in lots of different jobs and tasks.

Members volunteer and are supported to provide services to other vulnerable people living in their local community. They help with practical tasks in and around the home that the person is struggling to complete themselves. Common tasks include light housework, gardening, dog walking, shopping or simply befriending. The members have recently elected to do small jobs for local charities free of charge which brings a wealth of benefits. They also support other local voluntary groups in their promotional activities gaining from the social inclusion and friendships this brings.

We are a supported employment services that supports people with a disability in to paid employment .
We believe with the right support, anyone can have a job. We run award-winning supported employment schemes right across Buckinghamshire.
We support clients to develop key skills which enable them to find paid employment. We do this via:
· Apprenticeship schemes (including chefs, cleaners and gardeners)
· A busy café
· A working farm which attracts thousands of visitors each month
· A garden centre in the picturesque Great Missenden
· A gardening and maintenance service, offering practical skills development
We have great links with many well-known companies too to help you find the right job.
The team overseeing this vital transition is called Back2Base.
Our teams will build confidence and when clients are ready to move on, they offer personal support to help you find job opportunities which are right for you and match any aspirations.

name: Hillingdon- Rural Activities Garden Centre (RAGC) and the Rural Tea Rooms.
description_full:
The RAGC provides educational, supported employment and volunteering opportunities for residents with additional needs.
The RAGC produces a large range of bedding plants, shrubs and herbaceous plants, plus seasonal fruit, vegetables and free range eggs from chickens reared on site.
Also on the site and managed by Hillingdon Autistic Care & Support, are the Rural Tea Rooms. Here, they run the ‘Stepping Stones to Life skills and Employment’ project for young people affected by autism spectrum and learning disabilities. The aim of the project is to provide training and experience in a variety of areas which include Hospitality, Catering, Social Skills and Money Management Skills. All proceeds from the Rural Tea Rooms will be re-invested to support our learners.
http://www.hacs.org.uk/our-services/rural-tea-rooms/

About the Independent Lifestyles Service.
This is a support service for individuals with an Autistic Spectrum Condition (ASC) living in the community. It offers individualised support packages based upon the individuals needs. The service has been running for over 10 years.
The service operates across South East London, offering ASC specific, needs led, person centred support and:
- Is registered as a Personal Care Service with the Care Quality Commission
- Offers flexible, person centred support, seven days a week
- Offers support with daily skills such as budgeting, shopping, cooking and cleaning
- Helps to motivate individuals with personal care and developing self esteem
- Offers support to develop social and leisure activities and interests
- Offers support to access educational and work opportunities
- Supports individuals, with great success, in supported living accommodation. There has been staggering progress made by several of our supported living tenants.

How we support autistic people
Everyone has different likes and dislikes, needs and wishes. So, we put you at the centre of your support and build our support around you – not the other way round.
We will get to know you and to understand how you communicate and experience the world so that we can find out what support you need and what you want to do in your life. We will also get to know your routines, likes and dislikes so that we can help you to become more independent and live a fulfilling and happy life.
We can provide you with as much or as little support as you want, in many different ways such as:
-In your own home, in your family home or in shared accommodation.
-With your personal care, housework and finances.
-With employment, at college or university.
-Getting out and about.
-Going on holiday or pursuing your hobbies and pastimes.

website: http://www.ellingham.org.uk
About Ellingham Employment Services
Ellingham Employment Services Ltd. is a registered charity and company limited by guarantee, established in 1984.
We support people with disabilities within two main areas of speciality:
-Day Opportunities
-Employment Programmes
Ellingham are pioneering meaningful and empowering services for people with learning disabilities. Our I Can Ventures day opportunity service is a bold new vision. We want to do away with the mediocrity of traditional day services. Why should people with learning disabilities accept second best when choosing services?
We support individuals to learn and develop vocational and life skills, whilst enjoying social interaction and the chance to participate in fun, positive and constructive activities.
Employment Programmes
We work with people with a wide range of disabilities that may make it difficult for them to find employment and run programmes designed to assist them into work. In addition we provide advice and support to employers to help them employ and retain people with disabilities.
-Work Choice
-Work Programme
-Work Smart
-Access to Work
We are experts in this field of work, with a team of dedicated professionals who are experienced in integrating people with disabilities into the workplace.

name: Richmond- Choice Support
description_full:
Where we meet
We can meet you at a location that is convenient for you. Whether it's a cafe, library or college, we'll make sure it's a place you feel comfortable to talk.
Need support?
You can contact us directly or speak to your Learning Disability or Mental Health Team.
https://www.choicesupport.org.uk/find-support/find-support-near-you/richmond-wandsworth
How we can help
We will work with you to write an individual support plan with goals and timescales.
We will look at your work goals and provide support to develop your skills. This could mean doing work trials, work experience, education, training or voluntary work.
We run job clubs so that you can meet others who may be looking for work.
We will signpost you to organisations or resources to support particular needs.
We will provide flexible support depending on where you are on your employment journey.

Avenues works with young people and adults with autism who have complex needs or behaviour that challenges. We can support people at home, to get involved in life outside the home, or, where appropriate for some adults, in residential settings.
Because everyone has different support needs and their reasons for seeking support vary, we start by building relationships with people based on trust and understanding, including with family members. People with autism often say that they can’t make sense of the world and feel like ‘tourists’ in day to day situations. We support people to feel confident about taking more control so they can make choices about their life, including social, leisure and learning or employment opportunities.

name:

Carlton Centre
description_full:
Based in Sidcup, we provide a wide range of opportunities for adults with autism and learning disabilities both at the centre itself and in the community.
Join Carlton
Get in touch
We help people to learn new skills and increase their confidence, through activity sessions at the centre or at community venues. We have minibuses to get to some of the venues or we use public transport.
Can we support you?
Yes, as long as you are over 18 years old and have autism or learning disabilities. You will need to pay for the activities and you can do that if you have your own funding or if you have been assessed for funding through your local council.
https://www.choicesupport.org.uk/find-support/find-support-near-you/carlton
Our activities
We decide the activities based on your feedback and your needs, and so that you can try new experiences.
We have a full programme of activities from cooking and computer skills to drama and drum beats. You will have a timetable of activities to choose from.
There is a small extra charge for some of our activities and we will tell you this when you join us.

name:
Wandsworth- Share Community
Individual placement and support
description_full:
Individual placement and support - helping you find the right job
Do you live in Wandsworth?
Do you want to find a job?
If your answer to all of these questions is YES, Share's Employment Specialist can help you.
What we offer:
-Benefits advice
-A CV review and updating service
-Help with writing a covering letter and filling in application forms
-Help with your job search and advice on where to look for jobs
-Advice on how to prepare for interviews, including conducting mock interviews
-Links with potential employers in Wandsworth
We focus on the jobs you want and tailor job search sessions to meet your needs. You will receive support throughout the recruitment process to ensure you find the right job for you.
How to sign up
To take advantage of Share's FREE employment support service, you must be a Wandsworth resident with a mental health concern that is being accessed and managed by a Wandsworth community mental health team.
Ask your care coordinator or key worker to refer you to Share’s Employment Specialist, Hannah Christian, email:

Westminster Kingsway College offers provision for 16-24 year old students with dedicated courses for students with learning disabilities and/or difficulties. These courses are designed to meet the requirements of the new Foundation Learning Framework and include an emphasis on personal and social development, life and money skills and employability skills where relevant to the student.
Students who apply for a course will undertake an assessment to make sure that they are on the right course to meet their learning needs with support provided to make sure that we help them succeed on their chosen course. Students who successfully complete the course could progress to a higher level or to voluntary or supported work.

name: Asperger London Area Group -ALAG
description_full:
ALAG runs a monthly support group for adults on the autistic spectrum living in the Greater London area although those from elsewhere are welcome. It is a chance to meet others to talk about common issues and to support and help each other. The first half-hour of the meeting is spent socialising and having refreshments. This is followed by the whole group sitting down together to discuss an agreed topic or to hear from different speakers who offer advice and guidance on issues relating to autism. These topics are planned in advance and any member can suggest a future topic.
The group meets on the first Tuesday of every month in Islington from 6.30pm to 8.30pm. Booking in advance for new attendees is essential. There is a small entrance fee of £3 (members) or £4 (non-members) to cover the cost of refreshments, including a vegetarian option.
Email: info@alag.org.uk
Telephone: 07435 560 568
Website: www.alag.org.uk
Registered Charity address:
ALAG
c/o Disability Action in Islington
90–92 Upper Street
London N1 0NP
(visitors by appointment ONLY)

name:
Autistic Inclusive Meets(AIM)
Autistic Community Group 
for Adults, children and carers
description_full:
An truly inclusive autism association
AIM Autistic Community Group
We provide social opportunities for both adults and children along with support for parents and carers aiding families and individuals to access the community in accepting inclusive circumstances.
We currently run two groups one is every Tuesday 4-6pm at Brook Hill Children’s Centre, Brook Hill Road, Woolwich, London SE18 6UZ,
& every Friday 10-12am at Trinity Centre, 165 Burrage Road, Plumstead, London SE18 7JW.
Refreshments inclusive of price, tea, coffee, biscuits, fruit and squash.
There is no age limit on our two groups and siblings are welcome .
There is sensory play provided each week to aid and stimulate the tactile, auditory, visual and olfactory senses including a home made play dough that is safe for oral seekers.
Referral not necessary pre diagnosis welcome.
Brook Hill group is £2 per family and Trinity is £3.
For more info email autisticinclusivemeets@gmail.com

description_full: https://www.meetup.com/Neurodiversity-Professional-Group/
full_address: This group is intended for anyone who has ADHD, Autism, Dyslexia, Dyspraxia, Dyscalculia, Dysgraphia, and Tourette's syndrome within UK Professions. This is consistent with ACAS's view on Neurodiversity (http://www.acas.org.uk/index.aspx?articleid=6676) however, given this is a new concept, membership will be open to other conditions that can be considered Neurodivergent. The aim of this group twofold: (ii) to create greater connections within our community and (ii) to consider how we can address some of the challenges we face. Events to connect and network will be the main focus (details will be made available to members).

Fair Play Barnet proudly provides a range of different activities for disabled children and young people throughout the year.
Enabling hours: We provide 1-1 support playworkers to enable your child or young person to attend mainstream play/leisure activities.
Our family focused project provides families with opportunities to take part in a variety of events that will engage and support your family. From family trips out to sporting sessions, dance and art workshops or drop in coffee mornings, there’s something for everyone!
Swimming: On Saturdays during termtime we provide support for small groups of children and young people to enjoy a fun swimming session at Finchley lido.
As well as being a short break commissioned by London Borough Barnet 0-25 service you can also purchase enabling hours and swimming directly from Fair Play Barnet.

name: Bexley Voice Parent Carer Forum
description_full:
We are a group of voluntary parents/carers of children with disabilities, special or additional needs who live in Bexley, Kent. The ages of the children and young people we represent are between 0-25.
We proudly became a registered charity in 2011.
Postal Address:
BEXLEY VOICE
C/O SEND HUB -
NORMANDY CHILDRENS CENTRE
FAIRFORD AVENUE, BARNEHURST,
KENT. DA7 6QP
Email: bexleyvoice@hotmail.co.uk
Telephone Helpline: 07512 409 936
(Please note this is answered by volunteers.
Please leave a message if unanswered and we will return your call as soon as possible.)
OUR AIMS
To represent families by providing a "voice" to express their views and opinions, to help improve the lives of children with special needs.
We do this by:
1. Attending meetings with Bexley Council for the development and provision of services for our children and young people.
2. Providing a central parent consultation point for Bexley Council and service providers.
3. Proactively providing ongoing feedback to service providers on performance.
4. Raising awareness of the impact of a wide range of special needs on family life.
5. Providing views, based on personal experiences and canvassed opinion, ensuring that all Parents and Carers, whatever their background, have the opportunity to have their opinions represented.
WHAT DO WE DO?
Bexley Voice:
-Attend regular meetings with service providers, such as Bexley Council, providing feedback to them about the needs that our children have.
-Provide a central consultation point for any organisation that needs advice on children or young people with special needs and/or disabilities.
-Signpost parents and carers who need contact information for local and national organisations, groups and charities.
-Have visited over 40 schools in Bexley as part of our School Visit Programme.
-Organised 3 Parent/Carer Conferences and over 30 parent and carer workshops on many different subjects such as the SEN Reforms.
-Support families by telephone, email and via our large information website and secret Facebook group.
WHEN DO WE MEET?
Bexley Voice would like to invite you to our "Tea and Talk Parent/Carer Support" group. It is a good time to come along, meet our members, have an informal chat over a cup of tea and discuss any issues you may have. We can offer advice on various issues such as statements/EHC Plans, transport, transition, schools, benefits and activities.

name: Camden special parents forum
description_full:
Camden special parents forum
As part of our parent and family support services, Camden Special Parents’ Forum is a growing network of over 250 parents and carers of 0-25 year olds with special education needs and disabilities.
The forum runs meetings and training workshops for parents to help them to access the information and skills they need to support their families. Anyone who is looking after a child or young person with special needs (who lives or goes to school in Camden) is welcome to join the Forum, either as a regular or occasional visitor. We will make you welcome and you can be involved in our activities as much as you want.

name: Chalfont St Peter Youth Centre
description_full:
Holds a weekly session for young people with ASD from 7-8pm on Tuesdays during term time.
The sessions are supervised by trained adults but benefit enormously from a team of trained young volunteer helpers. These helpers are on hand for each session to interact with the young people attending, encouraging play and social skills by modelling behaviour.
The youth club has a state of the art, fenced outdoor AstroTurf sports court enabling the young people to play football, basketball etc. Also air hockey, pool, table football, arts, craft and Lego are available at each session.
The youth centre is the holder of the Queen's Award, the MBE for voluntary services which it received in 2013.
Please call the office on 01753 883712 or email office@cspyouthcentre.org.uk for further information.
Tithe Barn
Sandy Rise
Chalfont St Peter
Bucks SL9 9TR
full_address: The club is aimed at young people from Year 5-Year 8 approximately and has been successfully running for around 2 years. It aims to provide a safe and calmer environment for young people who wish to socialise but perhaps struggle to cope with larger, louder environments.
content:
Holds a weekly session for young people with ASD from 7-8pm on Tuesdays during term time.
The club is aimed at young people from Year 5-Year 8 approximately and has been successfully running for around 2 years. It aims to provide a safe and calmer environment for young people who wish to socialise but perhaps struggle to cope with larger, louder environments.
The sessions are supervised by trained adults but benefit enormously from a team of trained young volunteer helpers. These helpers are on hand for each session to interact with the young people attending, encouraging play and social skills by modelling behaviour.
The youth club has a state of the art, fenced outdoor AstroTurf sports court enabling the young people to play football, basketball etc. Also air hockey, pool, table football, arts, craft and Lego are available at each session.
The youth centre is the holder of the Queen's Award, the MBE for voluntary services which it received in 2013.
 
Please call the office on 01753 883712 or email office@cspyouthcentre.org.uk for further information.

Tithe Barn
Sandy Rise
Chalfont St Peter
Bucks SL9 9TR

name: City of London Parent Carer Forum
description_full:
City Parents & Carers
We are working with the City of London and the City and Hackney CCG to ensure that our voices are heard when decisions are being made which may affect our children and young people. We are represented on the City of London’s SEND Programme Board.
Parents and carers of children and young people who have an Education Health and Care plan have been meeting once a term with local authority officers to talk about the services we would like to see for our children and young people. We are developing and growing to include parents and carers of all children and young people with SEND regardless of whether they have an EHC plan.
As we grow we will be:
• Organising activities for our children and families
• Organising events and training days
• Linking with forums in other parts of the UK to share ideas

City Parents & Carers
The Parent Carer Forum is for parents and carers of all children and young people in the City of London who have special educational needs and or a disability (SEND).

We are working with the City of London and the City and Hackney CCG to ensure that our voices are heard when decisions are being made which may affect our children and young people. We are represented on the City of London’s SEND Programme Board.

Parents and carers of children and young people who have an Education Health and Care plan have been meeting once a term with local authority officers to talk about the services we would like to see for our children and young people. We are developing and growing to include parents and carers of all children and young people with SEND regardless of whether they have an EHC plan.

As we grow we will be:
• Organising activities for our children and families
• Organising events and training days
• Linking with forums in other parts of the UK to share ideas

name: Croydon- Jubilee Chill’n’Chat Group
description_full:
Chill’n’Chat Special is weekly group for parents and carers of children with special needs. We
recognise that all children are unique and they all develop and learn at different rates. Your
The Programme – To make this group as relevant as possible for those attending, the
programme is decided by the parents. We like to have a mixture of speakers from other
organisations, (National Autistic Society, Parents in Partnership, Paediatric Physiotherapists
and Parent Partnerships), parenting topics (building self-esteem, managing behaviour,
parenting styles etc.) and treats (crafts, card making, manicures, hand massages etc.).
Current Venues:-
Woodlands Children’s Centre, Selsdon CR2 8HD Tuesday morning 9.30-11.30
Kensington Avenue Children’s Centre, CR7 8BT – Wednesday afternoons 1.00-2.45
These groups meet term time only. Creche places may be available if booked in advance.
Jubilee Parenting - have been supporting parents in Croydon for about 12 years. We run a
range of parenting courses alongside our weekly Chill’n’Chats. We aim to provide information,
practical support and some relaxation time for parents who are often under pressure.
PRISM – is a consortium of organisations working together to support parents of children
with special needs.
If you would like more information or to be added to our email circulation, please contact
Jane Carey or Rachel Cummings
email: Jane.carey@croydon-jubilee.co.uk / Rachel.cummings@croydon-jubilee.co.uk

city: child does not need to have a formal diagnosis for you to attend these sessions.

The Leslie Park Project provides day activities for younger aged adults (18-40 years of age).

The Carer’s Assessment and Support Service (CASS) offers help and advice to carers aged over 50 who look after someone with learning disability.

A meeting for people who have a learning disability and their carers – often attended by health professionals, council members and speakers.

name: The Larches Trust -Edgware
description_full:
The Larches Community is a registered charity established in 1995 by a group of families to create opportunities for young people and adults with learning disabilities and/or Autism and Asperger's syndrome.
www.larchescommunity.org.uk
Tel: +44 20 8905 6333
Objectives
1.Education and Training: To increase the skills base of young people and adults with learning disabilities through education and training so that they can enjoy greater independence in their lives.
2.Social and Recreational: To increase the quality of life of young people and adults with learning disabilities through social and recreational activities.
3.Social Enterprise: To become increasingly financially independent as an organisation by developing social enterprises and micro businesses.
4.Supported Living: To give people with learning disabilities the opportunity to live independent lives in the heart of the community by establishing supported living homes.
5.Community Inclusion: To combat prejudice within the mainstream community by increasing understanding of learning disabilities and autism.

This is a service providing respite to parents and carers supporting learning-disabled adults living at home in the London Borough of Enfield. The service is currently free at the point of delivery, being funded through the Enfield Carers’ Grant.

name: Enfield-Our Voice
description_full:
We are a parent–led organisation, working with parents and carers, as well as the statutory and voluntary sectors.
Tel: 07516 662315
Email: info @ ourvoiceenfield.org.uk
Aims
Our Voice aims to improve services for children and young people with disabilities or special educational needs in the borough of Enfield.
To do this we:
We negotiate representation on decision-making bodies
We invite service providers to consult us on local and national issues affecting our children and seek a can-do approach from them
We ask children, parents and carers to tell us about common concerns
We inform & support families though our newsletters, website, workshops and meetings
We campaign to change attitudes and remove barriers
We monitor changes in services, lobby for improvements and recognise and promote good practices
We help empower all parents of children with special needs and disabilities
We offer the following services:
-Family Network support help line
-Disability Network Meetings
-Information Newsletters
-Consultancy Services; e.g. running Parent consultations, or providing Training for Parents and Professionals

On club holidays they take part in rock climbing, abseiling, canoeing, dry slope skiing, caving and there is a lively programme of outings and events.

The club is run entirely by volunteers with a membership of around 120 young people.

name: Essex- Uniting Friends
description_full:
Our services cover Redbridge, Newham, Havering, Waltham Forest, Barking and Dagenham and Essex.
Our vision is for a world which recognises values and celebrates the lives and equal participation of people with a learning disability in society.
Our purpose is to provide opportunities for people to take part in social and educational activities in order to express their unique identities and develop their skills to reach their full potential in the community.
We are a user-led service, which ensures that adults with a learning disability take a leading role in defining and running their own provision.
See the website for activities such as our holidays, nightclub, allotment, cafe, radio show and more.
https://www.unitingfriends.org.uk/

name: Greenwich Mencap -Activities for Learning Disabilities and ADHD support group   ‘Greenlights’
description_full:
Greenwich Mencap run a range of social activities for people with a learning disability throughout the year. On a monthly basis we run two varying social activities, as well as a weekly lunchtime drop-in where adults with a learning disability can get together and socialise.
Committee Plus, our steering group of adults with a learning disability, also organises events and consultations several times throughout the year.
Activities Page:
http://www.greenwichmencap.org.uk/?page_id=61
Mencap runs a roving ADHD support group in locations around Greenwich on the last Friday of every month. The group offers parents of children and young people with ADHD a chance to meet other parents and carers and hear guest speakers present on special topics of interest such as medication and behavioural support.
For more information on times and locations:
Call the Mencap ‘Greenlights’ team on 0208 305 2245
or email rosanna@greenwichmencap.org.uk
Website: http://www.greenwichmencap.org.uk
full_address: We also run a monthly Games night with giant Jenga, picture bingo, a Nintendo Wii, pool, giant connect four, cards and ten-pin bowling.
content:
Greenwich Mencap run a range of social activities for people with a learning disability throughout the year. On a monthly basis we run two varying social activities, as well as a weekly lunchtime drop-in where adults with a learning disability can get together and socialise.

We also run a monthly Games night with giant Jenga, picture bingo, a Nintendo Wii, pool, giant connect four, cards and ten-pin bowling.

Committee Plus, our steering group of adults with a learning disability, also organises events and consultations several times throughout the year.

name: Greenwich Toy & Leisure Library

Greenwich Toy & Leisure Library Association is a toy library for children with special needs and their families/carers. Established in 1973 the organisation aims to support them by:-
Providing therapeutic, educational, stimulating toys and equipment
Promoting play and recreation in their development
Encouraging the development of self-advocacy skills
Educating parents and carers in the skills and knowledge they need to support and develop their children
Raising the awareness of the development needs of children and young people with disabilities
A toy library of 7,000 items
Home visiting support to parents and carers
Holidays and outings
Visit the Sensory Studio

name: Challengers -Guildford Playscheme
full_address: Challengers is an independent children's charity that, for more than 30 years, has developed and delivered inclusive play and leisure for disabled children and young people across Surrey, Hampshire and increasingly the surrounding areas. The charity is based in Guildford (at 'Challengers Guildford Playcentre', our headquarters and one of our full time playcentres). These services were delivered at Challengers Guildford and Farnham Centres as well as schemes based at schools and community youth centres all through the year.

name:
Hackney- KEEN 
One-to-one support,
Sports and activity sessions
description_full:
KEEN London is the only charity in London providing one-to-one support at free sports and activity sessions for children with additional needs or disabilities. We believe no child should miss out on the benefits of sports and physical activity. With the support of our amazing volunteers, our weekly sessions give our athletes the chance to play games, make friends and, most importantly, have fun!
At our sessions, each athlete is paired with a trained volunteer coach for individual support. This means they benefit from one-to-one attention and care. It also means their parents can leave their children in a secure and safe environment.
For families, volunteers, supporters and athletes, this means the KEEN family is one of a kind: welcoming, safe, supportive, rewarding and fun.

We run inclusive after school clubs and holiday playschemes where disabled and non-disabled children play together. Markfield’s highly trained staff are experienced in including children with a wide range of disabilities. We have youth clubs in the afternoon and evening with music, art, sport, films, where young people can socialise, relax and choose activities they enjoy. Our daytime and evening social clubs for adults with learning disabilities, autism or Aspergers Syndrome include dance, art, trips and discussion groups.
We have a wide range of family support services running at Markfield and outreach at other local venues. Our services aim to empower families to advocate for their rights, access services and participate in decision making. We focus on ways families can build supportive networks and get involved in the local community. We offer face to face and telephone advice, an information hub, free parent group coffee mornings and a monthly family club. We also community drop in sessions for all local families such as our Saturday Open Access adventure play and Under 5’s stay and play.

name: Harrow Mencap
description_full:
Contact:Julie Browne
Telephone:020 7423 7382
http://www.harrowmencap.org.uk
Description
Harrow Mencap is an independent charity - affiliated to National Mencap.
Started by parents and carers in the late 1940's we became a limited company in 1987. We have grown into a multi-project service provider to support people with learning disabilities and their families. We are proud of our local reputation as the primary campaigning organisation for people with learning disabilities in Harrow.
Our Purpose:-to work with and represent people with learning disabilities and their families and carers to ensure their needs, rights and aspirations are met.
Harrow Mencap's Children's Services offer a variety of play schemes to include Saturdays during term time; 10am-2pm and holiday clubs outside of term time; 10am-3pm weekdays. Saturday clubs operate from our Day Centre at Pinner Green and holiday clubs at Kingsley High School, Whittlesea Road, Harrow, HA3 6ND.
From September, we will also be offering after school clubs at Kinglsey School Harrow, Shaftesbury School Harrow and Woodlands School Edgware, 3.30pm to 5.30pm. We also offer transport as part of the service . Please contact Julie Browne for more information on:

Who we are and what we do?
ADD-vance is a registered charity set up in Hertfordshire in 1996 to support and empower adults, children and families with Autism, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) and related conditions in Hertfordshire.
Both these disorders make it difficult for children to succeed in school because of the resulting anxiety and behaviour difficulties. Parents often struggle with the challenging behaviour at home and can find that usual discipline methods are ineffective.
This is where we come in:
At ADD-vance we listen.
At ADD-vance we are non-judgmental.
At ADD-vance we give strategies, support and information.
At ADD-vance we provide a confidential, safe and friendly atmosphere where parents can speak freely (often for the first time) about their child/family issues.

description_full:
Palace for All provides services primarily for children with physical and or learning disabilities to enable them to reach their full potential.
Stay and Play
Palace has a large play space with lots of toys, craft activities, books and musical instruments. The play leader encourages constructive play and leads story time, drama sessions, singing and crafts. There is also plenty of opportunity for free play.
Therapies
Palace is a centre where children can get the therapies they need and where parents and carers can get support and relax in a friendly environment, learn from each other’s experiences and have a chat over a cup of tea.

-Speech and Language Therapy
-Cranial Osteopathy
-Reflexology

name: Kingston- Express CIC
description_full:
Express is a user-led organisation, self-referrals are welcomed and our support services have an open-door policy. Express reach over 1000 families, providing information, parent support groups, therapeutic groups, counselling services for parent/carers and a specific specialist counselling service for autistic young adults. Express also work with local universities and colleges to provide placements for dramatherapy, psychology and counselling students.
Express has now grown significantly to be the ‘go to’ place for families across the community. The long-term vision is to create a social environment where autistic young people and their families feel welcomed and secure; a place where young people can gain valuable social skills and work experience. A place for everyone.
To join our services please sign up to our eNewsletter and contact us www.expresscic.org.uk/contact-us to request our registration form.
Our facebook page also has lots of information including a link to join our closed forum for additional peer support.
Best wishes,
Annette & Tracey
Website www.expresscic.org.uk
Twitter @expresscic Facebook @expresscic
Donate www.localgiving.org/donation/expresscic

Drumbeat Outreach is a team of teachers and specialist advisers offering support, advice and training to children, families and professionals in Lewisham. We work across all mainstream educational settings, including Early Years, Primary and Secondary schools, and Post Sixteen provisions. Schools and Colleges can buy in support from the Outreach Team through a Service Level Agreement (SLA).
We run three licensed Earlybird, Earlybird Plus and Cygnet courses for the parents of children newly diagnosed on the autism spectrum. These courses run over a number of weeks and are facilitated by multi-disciplinary teams within Drumbeat and in collaboration with our partners in health.
We also run parent workshops and drop in groups across the school year in schools, children’s centres and at Drumbeat.

name:
Lewisham- SIGNAL 
(Support, Information, Guidance & News for Autism in Lewisham)
description_full:
Signal Family Support, C/o Hatcham Oak Children’s Centre
Brockley
London
SE4 2NX
Tel: 07946 632477
http://www.signalfamilysupport.org
SIGNAL (Support, Information, Guidance & News for Autism in Lewisham) is a local parent support group, based in the London Borough of Lewisham, set up a number of years ago by parents of children who have autism, Asperger syndrome or other autism spectrum disorders (ASDs).
The group hold monthly meetings which include talks from experts on a variety of topics related to autism, including current research and educational provision.
Coffee mornings, held in different members houses, are open to all members to exchange ideas, experiences and information or just have a chat.
The group produces a quarterly newsletter and runs a borrowing library with many specialist books, journals and articles, videos, and specially selected children’s books. It maintains links with local schools, hold conferences for schools and teachers, organises drama therapy and trampolining activities, provides parent speakers for local functions to raise autism awareness and fundraises within the local community and through grants.
It also runs a 16 plus and a sibling support club, has an annual party, and runs and subsidises holiday outings and other activities for its membership.

The autism spectrum team (part of the Special Educational Needs Service) run the Families in Luton Autism Group (FLAG). This was formed to address the needs of families affected by autism after an initial survey highlighted the parents’ wishes to link with families in similar circumstances, have access to a sympathetic listening ear and user friendly information about the autism spectrum. 

Each meeting provides parents with an opportunity to discuss issues with other families and carers, teachers, early years advisors and support workers – all of whom have had experience working with autistic children and those with Aspergers Syndrome.

We provide impartial advice and information on education issues to parents/carers of children who experience barriers to learning such as special educational needs (SEN), disability, exclusion or bullying. What do we do?: We can attend and support parents/carers in meetings about their child’s education, and offer support if the parent/carer wishes to request a statutory assessment. We also help with writing letters and reports. Who is it for?: Parents/carers of children and young people who are attending school in Newham, or who attend an out of borough school if the child is a Newham resident. What age group does it cover?: We have a statutory responsibility to work with children and young people up to 25 years for those who have a Statement of Special Educational Need.

name: Richmond- Knots Arts Youth Club
description_full:
Sessions are designed to empower young people to try new experiences and suggest what they would like to be doing whilst at the club. Each week we begin with a sharing of news which is followed by a healthy activity (e.g. sport or cookery) and then a creative or discussion activity (art/drama/planning a trip).
Powerstation Youth Centre
The Old Power Station,
121 Mortlake High St,
London SW14 8SN
email:  hello@knotsarts.com
Tel:  07947 212485

full_address: Knots Arts Saturday Youth Club takes place at the Powerstation Youth Club in Mortlake every Saturday during term time for young people aged 13 – 25 years old. The club is a thriving group of young people with social communication difficulties who love to try new things, meet new people and above all have fun.
city: meet new people and above all have fun.
content:
Knots Arts Saturday Youth Club takes place at the Powerstation Youth Club in Mortlake every Saturday during term time for young people aged 13 – 25 years old. The club is a thriving group of young people with social communication difficulties who love to try new things, meet new people and above all have fun.

Sessions are designed to empower young people to try new experiences and suggest what they would like to be doing whilst at the club. Each week we begin with a sharing of news which is followed by a healthy activity (e.g. sport or cookery) and then a creative or discussion activity (art/drama/planning a trip).

name:
Richmond- SENDIASS 
(Special Educational Needs and Disabilities Information Advice and Support Service)
description_full:
Richmond and Kingston Special Educational Needs and Disabilities Information Advice and Support Service or SENDIASS is a free, confidential and impartial service for parents and carers, children and young people (up to 25 years).
Richmond,
London,
TW92HP
Tel: 0208 831 6179
email: RichmondKingston@kids.org.uk
https://www.kids.org.uk/richmond-and-kingston-sendiass
The type and range of support offered includes:
-Information regarding the local offer, local policy and SEN/ disability law from independently trained staff.
-Advice for parent/carers, children and young people on gathering, understanding and interpreting information and applying it to their own situation.
-Personalisation of personal budgets
Information on the local authority’s processes for resolving disagreements, its complaints procedures and means of redress.
-Signposting children, young people and parents to alternative and additional sources of local and national information, advice and support.
Individual casework and representation where needed including support in attending meetings, contributing to assessments and reviews and participating in decisions about outcomes for the child or young person.
-Support for parents and young people in managing mediation and appeals to the SEND Tribunal.
full_address: 20 Windham Road,

name: Romford Autistic Group Support
description_full:
RAGS welcome families that may not meet the criteria for support elsewhere. We believe life is challenging enough without having to fight for your concerns to be heard because of a failure to tick someone’s box.
Autism is a condition that impacts on the whole family RAGS welcomes siblings to our activities because we believe they have special needs too.
We offer a telephone helpline, regular coffee mornings and sport/fun sessions. For ages 5-25+. Opening hours monday and wednesday during term time.

name:
Sidcup- Ken Boyce Centre
Choice Support
description_full:
Who do we support?
You must have funding in place to pay for the support. This could be funding through the London Borough of Bexley, through a personal budget or your own money.
How do I join the Ken Boyce Centre?
You can self-refer to us - just drop us an email or call us. You can get referred through social services at the London Borough of Bexley.
You may wish to visit us before you join. We'll give you a tour around the building and you'll get to meet the manager and the staff.
Parents group
Join our group for parents. We meet four times a year at the Ken Boyce Centre.
This is your opportunity to be more involved in what we do and be part of something special. You’ll be able to share advice, find out what’s going on and help organise fundraising events.
Email us to join our parents group
150 Rectory Lane
Footscray
Sidcup

full_address: Special Voices is a group of parents/carers of children and young people with special/additional needs. We are all volunteers working in partnership with voluntary and statutory organisations. The main aim of Special Voices is to raise awareness about the rights and needs of children/young people with special needs and to ensure that they and their families are consulted and involved in any decisions made during planning or developing services for them. Special Voices has a main steering group of voluntary parents/carers who have taken on the role of gathering your collective views and presenting them to service planners and decision makers in order to ensure that services are needs-led.

name:
South Bucks
 Rainbow Social Club
Bourne End
Westfield School
description_full:
This is a club that aims to enhance social interactions and well-being for children with autism. Meets at Westfield School on Monday evenings 5.00-6.45pm, term time only.
Suitable for children on the autism spectrum and their siblings; up to the age of 11.
We pride ourselves on offering a friendly environment for children and their families.
We aim to offer a wide variety of activities to promote positive interactions, self-esteem and well-being.
Activities include board games, arts & crafts, toys including trains sets, PE equipment in hall and many more things.
An opportunity to meet other parents and have a coffee.
Suggested voluntary donation of £4.00 per family includes a drink and a snack.
For more information, or to come along to the club, please contact:
Heather Wildsmith: Heather.Wildsmith@nas.org.uk
Westfield School,
Highfield Road,
Bourne End,
SL8 5BE

name: Southwark -Salmon Youth Centre
description_full:
Contact Matt Perkins for details,
Tel:  020 7237 3788
Email: matt.perkins@salmonyouthcentre.org
43 Old Jamaica Rd,  Bermondsey, Southwark.
London SE16 4TE,
DISABLED YOUNG PEOPLE
We want to make the Salmon Youth Centre a place where all young people can come and enjoy themselves – even if they have a disability that they think might make this difficult.
CLUBS
To start with, we have a specific Youth club for young people who have a disability and are aged 14+ (Friday, 3.30-5.30pm). In the club we do a range of activities (e.g. sport, games, drama, music, computers) and projects (e.g. making a DVD and volunteering at the Bermondsey Carnival).
As well as the clubs specifically for young people who have a disability, our whole programme of activities are available to you (see programme). Many of our staff have experience of working with young people who have a disability and we can arrange extra support to access sessions if you need it. We are also hoping to set up a parent and carer’s room on Saturday nights to support young people who have a disability to access the general 14+ session that happens between 8-10pm.
RESIDENTIALS AND TRIPS
We go on trips to places like Thorpe Park and 10 pin bowling. This year we are hoping to go on our first two summer residential holidays which are specifically for young people who have a disability.
NEW SCHEME
Connect2 is a new scheme which pairs up young people who have a disability with trained volunteers who take them out into the local community. As well as having fun and getting out and about, it is hoped that this scheme will help improve young people’s confidence and independence skills.

at universal settings such as youth services, after school clubs and children’s centres. The
youth clubs and after school clubs include football coaching, drama club, dance club and a
range of activities such as pool, video games and table tennis. The Inclusion Workers also offer
autism-specific support and training to mainstream youth clubs and after schools clubs to help
them fully support children and young people with autism.

The National Autistic Society,

Caring is more than just a job. It’s more than going for the prescriptions; it’s more than doing the shopping or helping the person you care for dress. It’s a commitment to someone you love. It’s supporting their emotional and physical needs and helping them retain their pride and dignity.

Our vision is that every unpaid carer in the areas in which we operate is recognised and leads a healthy, fulfilling life with control over their caring role.

name:

In-Deep Children’s Music Therapy
description_full:
The sessions will be supported by a group of trained volunteers and are run by a qualified, experienced therapist.
Alongside the sessions, In-Deep will be providing parents a place where they can meet and share their experiences with other parents and carers; as many parents who care for a child with special needs and/or disabilities can often find themselves feeling isolated, overwhelmed and alone.
Music therapy has long been shown to help children communicate and help develop speech and further physical, emotional and mental development.
If your child has learning difficulties or disabilities just bring them along to one of our sessions all children will be welcomed and catered for. For more information contact Emma Chapman by using the email:

name: Westminster Parents Participation Group
description_full:
We are a group of parent carer volunteers working together as Westminster Parent Participation Group (WPPG). This parent led group is for all Westminster parent and carers of children and adults with special educational needs and/or a disability (up to 25 years).
-We create a space at our drop-in sessions where parents can meet together, ask questions, talk and feel listened to.
-We can help you to access information, services and support.
-We run weekly drop-ins, information sessions and consultation events (term time only)
We meet every Thursday during school term time at our LDN living office between 10am-12pm. LDN Drop-in 389a Harrow Road, W9 3NA
We now also meet on Tuesdays between 10am -12pm at the Victoria Medical Centre, 29 Upper Tachbrook Street, SW1V 1SN
You are free to drop–in and meet parents/carers just like you and share your stories with them. We can also help guide you in the right direction to finding the right people to help you further.

name: Westminster Tresham Play Service - After School/Youth Club
description_full:
Tresham is a new purpose built play centre on the Lisson Green estate and play places are provided to support children with disabilities to be able to play, develop and have fun alongside local mainstream children accessing the service.
Tresham has a safe outdoor back area, a large multi purpose sports hall and a versatile play space offering art and craft, cooking, music and other creative play opportunities. The building has security to ensure that entering and exiting is closely monitored.
There is a team of staff available on site at all times and staff are experienced in and trained to understand and meet the needs of disabled children.

description_full:
An truly inclusive autism association
AIM Autistic Community Group
We provide social opportunities for both adults and children along with support for parents and carers aiding families and individuals to access the community in accepting inclusive circumstances.
We currently run two groups one is every Tuesday 4-6pm at Brook Hill Children’s Centre, Brook Hill Road, Woolwich, London SE18 6UZ,
& every Friday 10-12am at Trinity Centre, 165 Burrage Road, Plumstead, London SE18 7JW.
Refreshments inclusive of price, tea, coffee, biscuits, fruit and squash.
There is no age limit on our two groups and siblings are welcome .
There is sensory play provided each week to aid and stimulate the tactile, auditory, visual and olfactory senses including a home made play dough that is safe for oral seekers.
Referral not necessary pre diagnosis welcome.
Brook Hill group is £2 per family and Trinity is £3.
For more info email autisticinclusivemeets@gmail.com

name: Kent Autistic Trust - Living Services
description_full:
Living Services
Some of our homes are Registered with the Care Quality Commission and regular inspection reports are available on the Care Quality Commission website.
We also provide support for people from self-contained flats in shared accommodation with communal areas with the levels of staff determined by the individuals who live there. Staff can be shared to enable a higher overall level of support. This type of support is especially cost effective and flexible for people who want less support.
For people who are supported through Supporting People funding we have Steady State Contracts with Local Authorities and are required to adhere to a Quality Assessment Framework.
However a person chooses to get their support from the Trust we believe in an individual, personal approach. Providing sensitive support at the level determined by each individual’s need. We use person centred approaches and work with each person to find the right balance of positive aid for maximum independence, choice and determination. We believe that each person can tell us what they want in their own home and we work hard to ensure that we have all the mechanisms in place to make sure we hear what people tell us whatever their method of communication.
For more detailed information about our support services please visit this section of our website:
https://www.kentautistic.com/what-we-do/living-services/
Contact details:

name: London Transport Museum
description_full:
SEN mornings at the Museum
LTM Early explorers
Duration: 09.00 onwards (open to the general public from 10.00)
Tickets: Advanced booking is essential for both the Families and Schools morning sessions as places are limited. Please call 020 7565 7298 to book your place now. Children and young people under 18 go free; adults pay standard entry rates.
For families with Special Educational Needs, we open early twice a year. Enjoy the Museum at a quieter time, free from the general public and take part in one of our interactive workshops and demonstrations. Many of the sounds are switched off, making the Museum a peaceful place to be.
Explore our All Aboard family galleries in peace, try your hand at our bespoke craft activities or get hands on with genuine parts of transport history.

name: ‎Tower Hamlets -V&A Museum of Childhood
description_full:
V&A Museum of Childhood
London E2 9PA
https://www.vam.ac.uk/moc/
Museum opening hours
Open daily 10.00-17.45 (last admission 17.30)
Admission free.
For groups of more than 10 it is recommended to give notice.
Tel:  020 8983 5200
Visiting with an autistic child
https://www.vam.ac.uk/moc/learning/sen/visiting-autistic-child/
The Museum has worked with families of autistic children to create a pack for use in the Moving Toys Gallery and another for the Childhood Galleries. They include maps, toys to touch, activity suggestions and ear defenders. PECS symbols and a photo booklet are included to help with communication.
Packs are free, and available to borrow from the Information Desk for up to one hour. Please leave a form of ID e.g. a passport or a driving licence, or a £10 refundable deposit

name:

description_full:
WeSwim Barbican
If you have any general questions about swimming with WeSwim you can view them here.
Where?
Golden Lane Leisure Centre is based at Fann St, Fane Lane, Golden Lane Estate, London EC1Y 0SH, 0.2 miles from Barbican Station and is a multi-sport community sports centre.
There are steps or a pedestrian ramp to access to the leisure centre, with level access once inside. Access to the pool is via a ladder or chair hoist. Lockers require a padlock.
We have dedicated use of the 20m pool, where you will be supported by our team of friendly, enthusiastic and trained volunteers, providing a safe, private environment to swim in.
When?
The weekly session will be held on Wednesdays from 7pm to 8pm. After your swim there will be an opportunity to chat to other swimmers and volunteers over a well earned refreshments in the swimming pool reception.
Who?
The sessions and community at WeSwim Barbican will be led by a fabulous committee, who will also act as our safeguarding officers and will manage a team of volunteers to support our swimmers.
Cost?
We ask swimmers for a £2.00 contribution per session to help support the on-going operation of the club. If you have worries about being able to pay this on a regular basis please talk to tash@weswim.club.
https://weswim.club/barbican/

name: Sport- Crouch End WeSwim
description_full:
WeSwim Crouch End
If you have any general questions about swimming with WeSwim you can view them here and specific questions about Crouch End here.
Where?
Park Road Pools and Fitness is based in Crouch End on Park Rd, London N8 8JN. It is a multi-sport community sports centre between Hornsey and Highgate.
Park Road Pools & Fitness has fantastic disabled access to the building. There is level access through the leisure centre to the pool, changing rooms and the swimming pool so whatever your disability, confidence or swimming level we would love to support you in exploring the mental, physical and social benefits of swimming and being in the water.
Access to the water is via wide steps or a chair hoist. We have the use of two lanes of the 25m pool, where you will be supported by our team of friendly, enthusiastic and trained volunteers, providing a safe, private environment to swim in.
When?
The weekly session will be held on Mondays from 5.45pm – 7pm. After your swim there will be an opportunity to chat to other swimmers and volunteers over a well earned refreshments in the swimming pool reception.

“To work at foundation level to support disabled people to participate in a variety of sport, leisure and recreational activities in a formal or informal setting. name: Cycling -Bushy Park Hampton Crt
description_full:
Saturday 11.00am to 1.00pm
TW12 2EJ, United Kingdom
http://www.bikeworks.org.uk/
Companion Cycling is a scheme which enables people who are unable to ride “solo” cycles for whatever reason, to enjoy the pleasure of cycling with a companion in the delightful and traffic free surroundings of Bushy Park, adjacent to Hampton Court in South West London.Anyone, whatever their ability or mobility, can become a cyclist using one of our special cyclesand with the aid of a “pilot” who might be a carer, a friend, a family member or one of our volunteers.Our wide variety of special cycles are carefully selected to enable people to ride whatever their ability. These range from side-by-side pedal cycles suited to those with good mobility but limited co-ordination, through to a wheelchair ‘rickshaw’ suited to riders with severe mobility problems. In addition we have a range of tricycles with added safety features, a tandem and various conventional bicycles for accompanying riders. Our volunteers will help you to choose the cycle which is most suited to your needs, and will familiarise new riders and pilots with the cycles and the routes around the park.
full_address: Companion cycling – Bushy Park
content:

Companion Cycling is a scheme which enables people who are unable to ride “solo” cycles for whatever reason, to enjoy the pleasure of cycling with a companion in the delightful and traffic free surroundings of Bushy Park, adjacent to Hampton Court in South West London.Anyone, whatever their ability or mobility, can become a cyclist using one of our special cyclesand with the aid of a “pilot” who might be a carer, a friend, a family member or one of our volunteers.Our wide variety of special cycles are carefully selected to enable people to ride whatever their ability. These range from side-by-side pedal cycles suited to those with good mobility but limited co-ordination, through to a wheelchair ‘rickshaw’ suited to riders with severe mobility problems. In addition we have a range of tricycles with added safety features, a tandem and various conventional bicycles for accompanying riders. Our volunteers will help you to choose the cycle which is most suited to your needs, and will familiarise new riders and pilots with the cycles and the routes around the park.

name:
Sport- Allianz Park 
Hendon
Sarrie's Skills Club
Saracens Rugby Club
description_full:
This programme is the pioneer in itself as it is the first provision specifically for autism to be run by a professional sports teams in the UK. We use multi-sport sessions with each member having a tailored training programme in order to suit their specific needs.
The aim of the sessions are to improve the physical capability of each individual as well as using it as a vehicle to improve basic life skills such as social interaction, sleep and their behaviour. In order to achieve these aims each member will have at least 1 on 1 support which consists of both staff and volunteers who are all devoted and passionate in aiding towards the cause.
Whilst our members are down stairs blasting through their activities, we provide a respite area to parents and carers which takes place in our hospitality lounge, where they are able take a well-earned rest or share some of their stories with others.
If you would to know more information about this project, please contact: Benjamin Lawrence –DisAbility Sport Development Officer - Tel: 0203 675 7243 - Email: benjaminlawrence@saracens.net
http://www.saracens.com/foundation/inclusion/sarries-skill-club

Horizon Sports Club
description_full:
Horizon welcomes all children with a disability from the age of 4 years within South Buckinghamshire.
Horizon operates two sections within the club. Our junior section accommodates 4–11 year olds and provides one-one support from volunteer coaching assistants. Our youth section also has volunteers supporting them, if that is deemed appropriate.
Wycombe Leisure Centre
Marlow Hill, High Wycombe,
Buckinghamshire HP11 1TJ
http://www.horizonsportsclub.org.uk
Tel: 01494 349001
email: info@horizonsportsclub.org.uk
Activities
-Horizon Minis
Tuesday 4.00pm
This class formerly is aimed at members aged between 4 and 8 years old. It will give our members the opportunity to experience, learn and develop a wide range of skills and take part in a number of games with a specific focus on exercises to support their individual needs.
-Junior Swimming
Wednesday 6.30pm and 7.20pm
Horizon junior swimming classes present our members with the valuable opportunity to become confident and most importantly safe in the water. Our swimming teachers will work each week to develop the knowledge and skills required to achieve the ultimate outcome of independent swimming. His class is for age 7-13 years old.
-Junior Ball Skills
Wednesday 5.15pm and Thursday 6.00pm
This class is designed to allow our members the opportunity to further develop all skills associated with ball-based games. Each week will have a specific sports focus ranging from football to cricket and everything in between. It will not just be the skills of the game but also the rules that will be taught. Each session will conclude with small-sides games to encourage team work and introduce members to more competitive situations. This class is aimed at ages 7-13.

name:
Sport- Kensington & Chelsea
Disability Sports Coach Club
description_full:
The club takes place on Saturday mornings with football, boccia, basketball and cricket the most popular sports!
During the year, the club will have specialist coaching visits from The Golf Trust, Westway tennis coaches and The Wheelchair Dance Association. The club also has access to a new cardio wall.
There is a lot of friendly competition at the club and many welcoming faces for any new people joining the session. Come join us!

At LAGAD our teachers are former Olympians, World and European competitors and other top performers who show great passion and energy for their discipline. Our highly experienced coaches and dance teachers are fully qualified, DBS checked and insured.

name: Sport -Old Street Swimming Pool
description_full:
Our session at Old Street in Islington, London, is the first WeSwim club. It has excellent facilities and the use of two pools to meet people’s differing needs.
If you have any general questions about swimming with WeSwim you can view them here and specific questions related to WeSwim Old Street here.
Where?
Ironmonger Row Baths, at 1 Norman St EC1V 3AA has fantastic disabled access to the building, changing rooms and the swimming pool so whatever your disability, confidence or swimming level we would love to support you in exploring the mental, physical and social benefits of swimming and being in the water.
We have dedicated use of the 15m teaching pool & 2 lanes of the main pool at Ironmonger Row Baths, where you will be supported by our team of friendly, enthusiastic and trained volunteers, providing a safe, private environment to swim in.
When?
The weekly session are currently held on Tuesdays between 7.15 – 8.30pm. After your swim there will be an opportunity to chat to other swimmers and volunteers over a well earned refreshments in the swimming pool reception.
https://weswim.club/old-street/

Every Wednesday 1pm until 3pm. Alternate Saturdays between 1pm and 3pm. On Saturdays, there is an under 16s club between 12pm and 1pm.

We meet at the Lock House gate entrance which you can access by Parnell Road.

description_full:

Kent,

07967 346060
Email Address
kay.salter@btopenworld.com
Websites
www.falconspartak.com
Inclusive sessions include disabled participants, ensuring that additional help and support is provided when and if required. Dedicated sessions follow the inclusive gym class but allowing for 1:1 coach support work where it is required. Use of special adapted equipment as well as mainstream gymnastics equipment is used to enable all ability levels to participate and access gymnastics. Falcon Spartak allow carers and parents to assist in the gym. They can leave as and when they are happy to. Gymnasts can also be given a buddy to work with. All new gymnasts are allowed 3 taster sessions before the final decision is taken to join the club. this allows the gymnast and parent/carers time to decide if they like the club. Disabled Gymnasts have the opportunity to compete in regional and club competitions, displays, GymFusion, Disability National events in Rhythmic, Artistic and Trampolining. All coaches in the club have taken the disability add-on module (3 club coaches, 1 assistant coach, 1 trampoline coach and 1 senior club coach in Rhythmic) Club competitions take place before Christmas. The club also celebrates its 40th birthday in 2013 so there will be events to celebrate this. All events held at the club are fully inclusive and gymnasts from dedicated sessions are also encouraged to attend.
Sports Offered
Gymnastics
Impairments catered for
Amputee, Learning Disabilities, Visual Impairment.
When do we meet
Wednesday 6.30pm to 8.30pm, Thursday 6.30 to 8.30pm, Saturday 12.30pm to 2.30pm

name: Cycling -Sutton
description_full:
Monday 10am – 12pm
http://cyclingforall.org/cycling-sessions/
Come along and try out our cycling sessions on a Monday, Wednesday and Thursday mornings at David Weir Leisure Centre (Sutton Arena). Sessions are good fun, sociable and are a great way to get some exercise! We have a wide range of bikes, trikes and 4-wheelers and run two types of session on each of the days:- 10am – 11am: informal inclusive cycling sessions for adults who would like an opportunity to learn to ride, regain cycling confidence, improve cycling skills, socialise and get gentle exercise in safe off-road environment- 11am – 12noon: everybody is welcome, but from 11am we cater for groups of adults with a disability.

Tel: 020 7738 3478
email: info@ebonyhorseclub.org
full_address: Ebony Horse Club specialises in young people with emotional and behavioral difficulties, and welcomes those with special educational needs and some medical conditions. We are not a Riding for the Disabled Centre, but we do have a specialist instructor who is RDA-qualified. We do not have experience of all conditions and therefore assess our ability to accommodate each young person individually. If we are unable to accommodate a young person, we will recommend another centre with suitable expertise and facilities. The vast majority of those with special needs or disabilities are fully integrated within our regular classes and we currently have many riders on the autistic spectrum registered with us.
city: we will recommend another centre with suitable expertise and facilities. The vast majority of those with special needs or disabilities are fully integrated within our regular classes and we currently have many riders on the autistic spectrum registered with us.

name: Nightlife East London Bubble Club -Access to Nightlife for everyone
description_full:
The original Bubble Club project grew from out of a series of meetings held in 2005 as part of an think tank called "Have Your Say" where people with learning disabilities were given the chance to speak out about the injustices they were experiencing in their local communities. Some of the major concerns being raised were widespread experiences of social isolation and loneliness. People reported feeling unsafe to go out in the evenings and lacked any opportunity to socialise in the evening.
A number of these people went on to pioneer the possibilities of what high quality accessible nightlife could be and nearly 15 years later the Bubble Club is now a house hold name to many in East London and an institution people with learning disabilities across the country have come to know and love.
